policy_statement: Build additional desalination capacity: Kuwait produced around 3.85 MCM/d of desalinated water in 2016; eight additional plants were being commissioned and additional IWPP/plant projects are planned, including Al-Zour North (250 MGD), Al-Zour South (145 MGD), Al Khiran (125 MGD), and Doha (60 MGD), with further projects planned to be tendered as of 2019 (~285 MGD additional).
mechanism_description: Building and commissioning additional desalination plants increases desalination capacity, strengthening water supply reliability and resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/precipitation variability/salinization) → ↑ reliable potable water supply (via desalination) → ↓ exposure/vulnerability to water shortage
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Governments/utilities invest in, construct, and operate desalination plants (often including intake/outfall infrastructure and distribution connections) to augment municipal/industrial water supply.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Seawater/brackish water is treated (e.g., reverse osmosis/thermal) to produce potable water, creating a drought-independent supply source that is less sensitive to rainfall and river inflows.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced likelihood and severity of water supply interruptions and water stress during climate-exacerbated droughts and hydrologic variability, improving overall water security and resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improving resilience to climate-driven water scarcity by providing a more reliable freshwater supply during droughts and variable precipitation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: desalination capacity expansion most directly increases reliability of water supply under drought/variable precipitation, which is an adaptation (reduced vulnerability to a climate hazard).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: although desalination affects energy demand and can raise or lower emissions depending on the electricity mix, those mitigation/resource-efficiency effects are not the core shared outcome of “capacity expansion → water supply resilience”; the shared, primary outcome is resilience to water scarcity.  
3) Edge cases applied: some desalination projects are paired with renewable power or efficiency upgrades (potential mitigation co-benefits) or may worsen emissions if fossil-powered; however, these are conditional implementation details rather than the dominant mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy that expands desalination capacity to bolster supply reliability is fundamentally addressing water scarcity risk and thus fits Adaptation as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: whether a given policy also qualifies for mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., renewable-powered desalination, energy recovery) must be determined at the individual policy level with explicit evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “desalination expansion” policy is explicitly framed/implemented primarily to reduce emissions (e.g., replacing more carbon-intensive water imports/treatment) or is coupled to guaranteed renewable energy such that the dominant claimed benefit is emissions reduction, classification could shift at the policy level; however, for the canonical mechanism as stated (capacity expansion → resilience), Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: “Building desalination plants”; “produced around 3.85 MCM/d of desalinated water”; “Eight additional plants were being commissioned by the government”; “IWPP”; “planned to be tendered”; “additional capacity”; plant names and capacities “Al-Zour North…250 MGD… Al-Zour South…145 MGD… Al Khiran…125 MGD… Doha…60 MGD… ~285 MGD additional”
co_benefits: Potential public health protection and economic/industrial continuity from improved water reliability; potential employment/industrial development from large infrastructure projects.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement/PPP instrument due to “IWPP” and “tendered,” but the dominant mechanism in the text is capacity expansion via construction/commissioning (infrastructure investment).
